one_line_takeaway: LLM-ATTACK uses an LLM as the synonym generator (plus NER/pronoun and USE constraints) to craft word-level adversarial text that is markedly more valid and human-imperceptible than TEXTFOOLER/BAE, at the cost of a lower raw attack success rate.
key_finding: On raw automatic attack success LLM-ATTACK is WEAKER than the unconstrained baselines (success 25.5%/53.7%/42.3% on MR/IMDB/Yelp vs TEXTFOOLER 89.8%/98.7%/94.9% and BAE 59.2%/67.5%/58.8%); the paper openly acknowledges this gap. Its actual advantage is in example quality: in human evaluation LLM-ATTACK reaches 89.1% validity (near the 90.2% of original texts) with only 13.0% suspiciousness, versus TEXTFOOLER 78.3%/56.5% and BAE 77.2%/39.1%. When the same constraints are imposed on the baselines their attack success collapses (e.g. BAE on IMDB drops to 36.4%, below LLM-ATTACK's 53.7%), so under a fair, constraint-matched comparison LLM-ATTACK outperforms BAE. Human and GPT-4 rankings of naturalness broadly agree (LLM-ATTACK selected by 69.6% of human judgments and 50.0% of GPT-4), which the authors read as evidence GPT-4 can serve as an adversarial-example evaluator.
core_contribution: The paper proposes LLM-ATTACK, a two-stage word-level textual adversarial attack that fools fine-tuned text classifiers while keeping the perturbed examples valid and natural. Its own novelty (as distinct from cited baselines TEXTFOOLER and BAE) is to replace the heuristic synonym source (counter-fitted word embeddings or BERT-MLM) with an LLM (ChatGLM-Pro) prompted to return a ranked list of k synonyms that preserve semantics, syntax and grammar, plus two extra quality constraints: named-entity/pronoun preservation (via spaCy NER, with pronouns added to the stop-word list) and a Universal Sentence Encoder gate to reject malformed LLM outputs. The authors claim to be the first to (a) use LLMs to generate textual adversarial examples and (b) use an LLM (GPT-4) to evaluate adversarial-example quality.
threat_model: Black-box evasion attack on a trained NLP text classifier F: X->Y at inference time. The adversary perturbs a correctly-classified input x into x' so F predicts the wrong label, under imperceptibility constraints (semantic preservation, grammaticality, human undetectability). Notably the attack is not query-only/hard-label: the word-importance-ranking stage requires access to the target model's output probabilities/logits P(y_true|x) to score each word by the confidence drop when it is masked to [UNK], so it assumes a soft-label black-box (score-based) setting rather than pure decision-based access.
limitations: Attack success rate on automatic metrics trails the unconstrained baselines, so the method trades attack potency for stealth/validity. Evaluation is small-scale: 500 samples per dataset, only 2 human evaluators, and the k grid search used just 50 IMDB samples. Only a single victim architecture (BERT) and a single LLM synonym source (ChatGLM-Pro) are tested, limiting generalization. The 'black-box' claim is soft-label (needs output probabilities for word ranking), not hard-label. Using GPT-4 both as a proposed evaluator and to validate its own agreement with humans is somewhat circular. Adversarial training / robustness benefit of the generated examples is only stated as future work, not demonstrated.

datasets_used: Movie Review (MR): 9K train / 1K test, avg len 22, 2 classes
IMDB: 25K train / 25K test, avg len 280, 2 classes
Yelp Review Polarity: 560K train / 38K test, avg len 154, 2 classes
500 samples randomly drawn from each test set for attack evaluation
quantitative_results: Clean BERT accuracy: MR 87.8, IMDB 92.4, Yelp 97.0
LLM-ATTACK attack success rate: MR 25.5%, IMDB 53.7%, Yelp 42.3% (after-attack accuracy 65.4 / 42.8 / 56.0)
TEXTFOOLER (original) success: MR 89.8%, IMDB 98.7%, Yelp 94.9%; w/ constraints drops to 31.4% / 76.0% / 66.2%
BAE (original) success: MR 59.2%, IMDB 67.5%, Yelp 58.8%; w/ constraints drops to 16.2% / 36.4% / 21.4% (LLM-ATTACK beats constrained BAE on all three)
Human validity/suspiciousness: Original 90.2%/-, TEXTFOOLER(w/C) 78.3%/56.5%, BAE(w/C) 77.2%/39.1%, LLM-ATTACK 89.1%/13.0%
Naturalness (lowest detectability + highest grammaticality/meaning) selection rate: human TEXTFOOLER 18.5 / BAE 28.3 / LLM-ATTACK 69.6; GPT-4 37.0 / 32.6 / 50.0
Hyperparameters: max modification rate theta=0.4, max word-embedding distance 0.5, USE threshold 0.9, k=15 synonyms, ChatGLM-Pro temperature 0
baselines_compared: TEXTFOOLER (Jin et al. 2020; counter-fitted embedding synonyms) - original and constraint-matched settings
BAE (Garg & Ramakrishnan 2020; BERT-MLM replacement) - original and constraint-matched settings
